title: Make graph strongly connected
description: Minimum edges to add for SCC

examples.explanation: Step 1: Original graph has vertices 0,1,2 and edges 0→1, 1→2. Check strong connectivity: DFS from 0 reaches [0,1,2]; from 1 reaches [1,2]; from 2 reaches [2] only → not strongly connected. Step 2: Try adding 0 edges: same as above → fail. Step 3: Try adding 1 edge: possible edges are (0,0),(0,2),(1,0),(1,1),(2,0),(2,1),(2,2). Add (2,0): new edges: 0→1,1→2,2→0. Check: DFS from 0 reaches all; from 1 reaches all; from 2 reaches all → strongly connected. Minimum edges = 1.

Step 1: Original graph: edges 0→1,1→2,2→3. Check strong connectivity: DFS from 0 reaches [0,1,2,3]; from 1 reaches [1,2,3]; from 2 reaches [2,3]; from 3 reaches [3] → not strongly connected. Step 2: Try 0 edges → fail. Step 3: Try 1 edge: add (3,0). New edges: 0→1,1→2,2→3,3→0. Check: DFS from 0 reaches all; from 1 reaches all; from 2 reaches all; from 3 reaches all → strongly connected. Minimum edges = 1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(V^2) with DFS
approaches.summary: Try adding all possible edges between vertices and check for strong connectivity using DFS from each vertex, stopping when the graph becomes strongly connected. Use this for small graphs to understand the problem, but it's impractical for larger inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Minimum Edges to Make Graph Strongly Connected via Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution using brute force: try adding all possible edges between vertices and check for strong connectivity with DFS from each vertex.
ladders.explanation: This is the full implementation of the brute force approach to find the minimum number of edges needed to make a directed graph strongly connected. The intuition is straightforward: since we need to ensure every vertex can reach every other vertex, we can systematically try adding edges between all pairs of vertices (including self-loops and existing edges) until the graph becomes strongly connected. We use DFS from each vertex to check strong connectivity—if all vertices are reachable from every starting vertex, the graph is strongly connected. The algorithm works by iterating through all possible edge combinations, starting with 0 additional edges, then 1, then 2, etc., up to V*(V-1) edges (the maximum possible in a directed graph). For each combination, we add the edges to a copy of the original graph, run DFS from each vertex to check reachability, and stop at the first combination that yields a strongly connected graph. This approach is only practical for small graphs (V ≤ 10) due to its exponential nature.
ladders.codePattern: Use nested loops to generate edge combinations, copy the graph, add edges, and call the strong connectivity check function.

examples.trace: Step 1: Original graph has vertices 0,1,2 and edges 0→1, 1→2. Check strong connectivity: DFS from 0 reaches [0,1,2]; from 1 reaches [1,2]; from 2 reaches [2] only → not strongly connected. Step 2: Try adding 0 edges: same as above → fail. Step 3: Try adding 1 edge: possible edges are (0,0),(0,2),(1,0),(1,1),(2,0),(2,1),(2,2). Add (2,0): new edges: 0→1,1→2,2→0. Check: DFS from 0 reaches all; from 1 reaches all; from 2 reaches all → strongly connected. Minimum edges = 1.

Step 1: Original graph: edges 0→1,1→2,2→3. Check strong connectivity: DFS from 0 reaches [0,1,2,3]; from 1 reaches [1,2,3]; from 2 reaches [2,3]; from 3 reaches [3] → not strongly connected. Step 2: Try 0 edges → fail. Step 3: Try 1 edge: add (3,0). New edges: 0→1,1→2,2→3,3→0. Check: DFS from 0 reaches all; from 1 reaches all; from 2 reaches all; from 3 reaches all → strongly connected. Minimum edges = 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Checking Strong Connectivity with DFS
ladders.desc: Helper function to determine if a directed graph is strongly connected using DFS from each vertex.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine checks whether a directed graph is strongly connected, meaning every vertex can reach every other vertex. The intuition is that if we start DFS from any vertex and it visits all vertices, that only guarantees reachability from that starting point. For strong connectivity, we need to verify this from every vertex. The algorithm performs DFS from each vertex as the starting point and checks if all vertices are visited in each DFS run. If any DFS fails to visit all vertices, the graph is not strongly connected. Step-by-step: 1. For each vertex v in the graph: a. Initialize a visited array of size V set to False. b. Perform DFS from v, marking visited vertices. c. After DFS, check if all vertices are visited. If not, return False. 2. If all vertices pass, return True. Time complexity is O(V*(V+E)) because we run DFS V times, each taking O(V+E). Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array and recursion stack. Connection to L0: This function is called repeatedly in the brute force search to test each candidate graph. Common mistakes include not resetting the visited array for each DFS, incorrect DFS implementation for directed graphs, or early termination. Real code pattern: Use a recursive DFS function that traverses outgoing edges and marks visited nodes.

examples.trace: Step 1: Graph has edges 0→1,1→2,2→0. Step 2: DFS from 0: visited [0,1,2] → all visited. Step 3: DFS from 1: visited [1,2,0] → all visited. Step 4: DFS from 2: visited [2,0,1] → all visited. Step 5: All DFS runs visited all vertices → strongly connected.

Step 1: Graph has edges 0→1,1→2. Step 2: DFS from 0: visited [0,1,2] → all visited. Step 3: DFS from 1: visited [1,2] → vertex 0 not visited. Step 4: Since DFS from 1 did not visit all vertices, graph is not strongly connected.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Brute Force Search Over Edge Combinations
ladders.desc: Systematically try adding edges between vertices until the graph becomes strongly connected.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique of the brute force approach: generating all possible combinations of edges to add to the graph and testing each combination for strong connectivity. The intuition is that the minimum number of edges needed is at most V*(V-1) (all possible directed edges excluding self-loops), so we can exhaustively search from 0 to this maximum. The algorithm works by: 1. Start with k = 0 additional edges. 2. Generate all combinations of k edges from the set of all possible directed edges (V*V total, including self-loops and existing edges). 3. For each combination: a. Create a copy of the original graph. b. Add the k edges to the copy. c. Check if the copy is strongly connected using DFS. d. If yes, return k as the minimum. 4. If no combination for k works, increment k and repeat.
commonMistakes: Not stopping at the first valid k

examples.trace: Step 1: All possible edges: (0,0),(0,1),(1,0),(1,1). Original graph has edge (0,1). Step 2: For k=1, combinations: [(0,0)], [(0,1)], [(1,0)], [(1,1)]. Step 3: Test (0,0): add to graph → edges: (0,1),(0,0). Check strong connectivity: DFS from 0 visits [0,1]; from 1 visits [1] only → fail. Step 4: Test (0,1): edge already exists → same as original → fail. Step 5: Test (1,0): add to graph → edges: (0,1),(1,0). Check: DFS from 0 visits all; from 1 visits all → strongly connected. Return k=1.

Step 1: Original graph has no edges. Step 2: For k=0: no edges → not strongly connected. Step 3: For k=1: try edges like (0,1) → not strongly connected. Step 4: For k=2: try combination (0,1),(1,0). Add edges: graph has 0→1,1→0. Check: DFS from 0 visits [0,1]; from 1 visits [1,0]; from 2 visits [2] only → fail. Try other combinations until finding one that connects all vertices, e.g., (0,1),(1,2) might work with k=2? Actually, need at least 2 edges to connect 3 vertices in a directed cycle: (0,1),(1,2),(2,0) is 3 edges, but brute force will find minimal.

testCases.expected: false (need at least 2 edges for V=2 with no edges)

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Representation and Edge Addition
ladders.desc: Represent a directed graph and add edges dynamically for testing combinations.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves representing a directed graph in a way that allows efficient edge addition and DFS traversal. The intuition is that we need a flexible data structure to model the graph so we can easily modify it by adding edges during the brute force search. The most common representation is an adjacency list: an array of lists, where each index corresponds to a vertex and its list contains the vertices it points to. For edge addition, we simply append to the appropriate list. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an adjacency list of size V with empty lists. 2. Add original edges by appending to the lists. 3. To add a new edge (u,v), append v to the list at index u. 4. For DFS traversal, use this adjacency list to iterate over outgoing edges. Time complexity for adding an edge is O(1) amortized; space complexity is O(V + E) to store the lists. Connection to L2: This representation is used to create graph copies and add edges in the brute force search. Common mistakes include using adjacency matrix (inefficient for sparse graphs), not handling duplicate edges, or incorrect indexing. Real code pattern: Use a list of lists in Python, e.g., graph = [[] for _ in range(V)].

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): graph[0].append(1) → graph = [[1],[],[]]. Step 3: Add edge (1,2): graph[1].append(2) → graph = [[1],[2],[]]. Step 4: Add edge (2,0): graph[2].append(0) → graph = [[1],[2],[0]].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Depth-First Search (DFS) on a Directed Graph
ladders.desc: Traverse a directed graph using DFS to visit all reachable vertices from a starting point.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves traversing a directed graph from a given starting vertex to visit all vertices reachable via outgoing edges. The intuition is to explore as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking, which helps in checking reachability. The algorithm uses recursion or a stack: 1. Start at vertex v. 2. Mark v as visited. 3. For each neighbor u in the adjacency list of v: if u is not visited, recursively call DFS on u. This ensures all vertices reachable from v are visited. Time complexity is O(V + E) for a single DFS run, as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array and recursion stack. Connection to L3: DFS is performed on the graph representation to check reachability. Common mistakes include not marking visited nodes (leading to infinite loops), incorrect neighbor iteration, or stack overflow for large graphs. Real code pattern: Define a recursive function dfs(v) that marks visited[v] = True and loops over graph[v].

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 0, mark visited[0]=True. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: [1]. Step 3: DFS on 1: mark visited[1]=True. Step 4: Neighbors of 1: [2]. Step 5: DFS on 2: mark visited[2]=True. Step 6: Neighbors of 2: [] → backtrack. Step 7: All vertices visited.

Step 1: Start at 0, mark visited[0]=True. Step 2: Neighbors of 0: [1]. Step 3: DFS on 1: mark visited[1]=True. Step 4: Neighbors of 1: [] → backtrack. Step 5: Vertices 2 and 3 are not reachable from 0, so visited = [0,1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Directed Graphs and Reachability
ladders.desc: Understand directed graphs and the concept of one vertex being reachable from another.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: a directed graph consists of vertices (nodes) and directed edges (arrows) that go from one vertex to another. Reachability means there is a path from one vertex to another following the direction of the edges. For example, if there's an edge from A to B, then B is reachable from A. If there's a path A→B→C, then C is reachable from A. The intuition is that in a directed graph, edges have direction, so reachability is not symmetric (A might reach B, but B might not reach A). This is foundational for understanding strong connectivity, which requires mutual reachability between all pairs. Step-by-step: 1. Define vertices as points. 2. Define edges as ordered pairs (u,v) meaning an arrow from u to v. 3. A path is a sequence of edges where the end of one edge is the start of the next. 4. Vertex v is reachable from u if there exists a path from u to v. Time and space analysis are not applicable at this basic level. Connection to L4: Reachability is checked using DFS. Common mistakes include confusing directed with undirected graphs, or misunderstanding path direction. Real code pattern: Represent vertices as integers and edges as lists of pairs.

examples.input: Graph: vertices {0,1,2}, edges {(0,1),(1,2)}. Is 2 reachable from 0?
examples.trace: Step 1: Edge (0,1) means 0→1. Step 2: Edge (1,2) means 1→2. Step 3: Path: 0→1→2, so 2 is reachable from 0.
Step 1: From vertex 2, there are no outgoing edges. Step 2: No path exists from 2 to 0, so 0 is not reachable from 2.

testCases.input: Vertices {A,B}, edge (A,B). Is B reachable from A?
Vertices {A,B}, edge (A,B). Is A reachable from B?
